title: A Technical Follow-Up: Building an Ontology and Wiring It into Agentic AI Applications with Palantir Foundry and AIP - Substack

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity representing a real-world concept that can be wired into agentic AI workflows for autonomous operation

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology mechanism for defining executable operations that AI agents can invoke to modify objects and trigger workflows

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology relationship definition connecting Object Types, enabling agents to traverse the knowledge graph

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-registered computation that can be called by AI agents to perform logic, queries, or transformations

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform where the Ontology is built and managed, serving as the foundation for agentic AI applications

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's Artificial Intelligence Platform that enables wiring Ontology objects, actions, and functions into AI agent workflows

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Wiring
extractedEntities.description: The architectural pattern of connecting Ontology objects, actions, and functions to AI agents so they can operate autonomously on enterprise data

extractedEntities.name: Agentic AI
extractedEntities.description: Design pattern where AI agents autonomously perform tasks by invoking Ontology actions and functions based on real-world events

extractedEntities.name: Agentic AI Application
extractedEntities.description: Building autonomous AI applications that leverage the Ontology for structured decision-making and action execution

keyInsights: The Ontology in Palantir Foundry is not an academic taxonomy but a practical, operational wiring layer for connecting data to AI agent workflows
Agentic AI applications require three Ontology primitives wired together: Object Types for state, Action Types for mutation, and Functions for computation
AIP enables AI agents to autonomously invoke Ontology actions and functions rather than requiring human-in-the-loop for every decision

title: Palantir - the LLM Enabler for Enterprise - Convequity (Substack)

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: A standardized set of concepts and relationships that enables LLMs to connect the dots across enterprise data, serving as the bridge between raw data and knowledge graphs

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that provides consistent terminology and structure for LLM enterprise data understanding

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attribute on Object Types that enables fine-grained security controls like column-level masking for LLM access

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship connector between Object Types that helps LLMs understand how enterprise concepts relate to each other

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform that crafts custom ontologies per client with built-in governance, enabling secure LLM deployment

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform for operationalizing LLMs with ontology-guided training on enterprise data

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology SDK enabling programmatic access to Ontology objects for LLM integration

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Guided LLM Training
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where an ontology structures labeled enterprise data to accelerate LLM few-shot learning from zero-shot, enabling rapid domain-specific knowledge acquisition

extractedEntities.name: Knowledge Graph Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: The flow of data sources through ontology into knowledge graph for successful LLM implementation

extractedEntities.name: Fine-Grained Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism enabling column-level, row-level, and PII masking controls so LLMs access only authorized data while revealing metadata

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Ontology-provided audit trail showing data provenance for compliance and troubleshooting

extractedEntities.name: Enterprise LLM Enablement
extractedEntities.description: Using Palantir's ontology to transform general LLMs into enterprise-specific knowledge workers with governance guardrails

keyInsights: The data flow for LLM success is: Data sources -> Ontology -> Knowledge Graph -> LLM implementation; the ontology is the critical middle layer
Palantir's key differentiator is crafting custom ontologies per client with governance built into the knowledge structures, unlike competitors like Databricks
An ontology enables the shift from zero-shot to few-shot learning for enterprise LLMs by providing structured, labeled domain data
Palantir can mask underlying data while revealing metadata, allowing LLMs to reason about data structure without accessing sensitive content
Without an ontology, applying a general LLM to enterprise data is like an apprentice learning from mentors who all use different terminology

title: Deploying Full Spectrum AI in Days: How AIP Bootcamps Work - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: AIP

extractedEntities.name: AIP Bootcamp
extractedEntities.description: Immersive 1-5 day hands-on-keyboard sessions where participants go from zero to production AI use case, building on real data and real problems

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: The structured enterprise data layer through which real-world events are translated into prompts, enabling AI to go beyond chat to automation

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity representing enterprise concepts that AI agents use to understand and process real-world events

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Executable operation type that enables AI to automate processes beyond simple chat interactions

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Computation registered in the Ontology that provides intelligent primitives for feature-rich business applications

extractedEntities.name: Chat to Automation
extractedEntities.description: The progression from chat-style LLM interaction to event-driven AI automation through the Ontology, where prompts derive from real-world events not just user input

extractedEntities.name: Empirical AI Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of defining AI architecture through hands-on experimentation rather than theoretical planning, discovering optimal LLM configurations through production use cases

extractedEntities.name: Rapid AI Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Deploying production AI use cases in 1-5 days through intensive bootcamp sessions using AIP's integrated platform

keyInsights: AIP Bootcamps demonstrate that AI use cases can go from zero to production in 1-5 days when the platform comes fully integrated with the Ontology
The key mental model shift is from chat-style AI to automation: prompts derived from real-world events translated through the Ontology, not just user input
AI architecture decisions (how many LLMs, open source vs commercial, fine-tuning vs prompt engineering) should be determined empirically through production use cases, not theoretically
AIP participants discover that non-algorithmic compute potential of AI extends far beyond chat into process automation and intelligent business primitives

AI governance in Palantir follows three pillars aligned with the EU AI Act: Data Governance, Risk Management, and Record Keeping
Data governance is positioned as the cornerstone of AI governance, ensuring every stage from data collection to model deployment maintains quality standards
AIP provides integrated tooling for tracking, maintenance, and continuous monitoring of AI applications including LLMs and multi-modal AI models

title: How Palantir AIP Enables UNS for Industry AI - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform that enhances the Unified Namespace by adding a logic and action layer to the data-centric foundation, forming a decision-centric ontology

extractedEntities.name: Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Decision-centric layer that enriches the Unified Namespace framework with advanced analytics and AI, enabling integrated human-AI decision-making in industrial settings

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity representing industrial entities like machines, sensors, and production lines within the UNS hierarchy

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attribute on industrial Object Types capturing sensor readings, machine states, and operational parameters

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship between industrial entities following hierarchical structures like ISA-95 (Enterprise/Site/Area/Line/Cell)

extractedEntities.name: Unified Namespace
extractedEntities.description: Architecture pattern consolidating naming and organization of entities across all data sources into a common language with unique identifiers, following standards like ISA-95

extractedEntities.name: Industry AI Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Three-element pattern requiring Data (UNS aggregation), Logic (AI algorithms), and Action (automated responses) integrated in a coherent architecture

extractedEntities.name: Decision-Centric Ontology
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where the Ontology adds logic and action layers on top of data-centric UNS, enabling AI-powered decision-making in manufacturing environments

extractedEntities.name: ISA-95 Hierarchy
extractedEntities.description: Industrial standard for organizing manufacturing data: Enterprise/Site/Area/Line/Cell, commonly used with Sparkplug B topic namespaces over MQTT

extractedEntities.name: OT Data Protocols
extractedEntities.description: Real-time streaming protocols (OPC-UA, MQTT, Kafka) and non-streaming strategies (CDC, Data Virtualization, Scheduled Polling) for integrating operational technology data

extractedEntities.name: Manufacturing AI
extractedEntities.description: Applying AIP's ontology layer to manufacturing UNS for predictive maintenance, quality control, and automated production adjustments

keyInsights: Industry AI requires three integrated elements: Data (UNS aggregation from sensors/machines/databases), Logic (AI algorithms), and Action (automated responses) governed by a coherent architecture
Palantir AIP transforms a data-centric UNS into a decision-centric ontology by adding logic and action layers on top of the namespace foundation
The UNS follows ISA-95 hierarchical structure (Enterprise/Site/Area/Line/Cell) with Sparkplug B providing standardized MQTT topic namespaces
Non-streaming data integration from MES, ERP, and PLM systems uses three strategies: Scheduled Data Polling, Change Data Capture (CDC), and Data Virtualization
The Ontology must work across human teams, AI agents, devices, and environments in industrial settings

title: Security auditing - Audit log categories - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log Categories
extractedEntities.description: Standardized classification system for audit events that enables service-agnostic querying by high-level action types (dataLoad, dataExport, userLogin) instead of service-specific event names

extractedEntities.name: Audit.3 Schema
extractedEntities.description: Next-generation audit log schema that enforces mandatory category usage with guaranteed requestFields and resultFields structure for every audit event

extractedEntities.name: Audit.2 Schema
extractedEntities.description: Legacy audit log schema where category information is optional and request_params/result_params may not follow consistent structure

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform generating audit logs across all services, categorized for security monitoring and compliance

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool used to filter and analyze audit log export datasets for security monitoring workflows

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology operation type whose execution is captured in audit logs under standardized categories

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log Fields
extractedEntities.description: Structured parameters in audit.3: requestFields and resultFields (replacing audit.2's request_params and result_params) providing predictable structure for automated analysis

extractedEntities.name: Category-Based Security Monitoring
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of filtering audit logs by high-level categories (dataExport, dataLoad, userLogin) rather than enumerating service-specific event names, enabling future-proof queries

keyInsights: Audit.3 schema enforces that every audit log must specify at least one category with explicitly defined requestFields and resultFields, unlike optional categories in audit.2
Audit log categories solve the problem of needing to enumerate hundreds of service-specific event names by abstracting to high-level actions like dataExport, dataLoad, and userLogin
Category-based monitoring is future-proof: new export capabilities automatically log with the dataExport category without requiring query updates
Field naming changed between schemas: audit.3 uses requestFields/resultFields while audit.2 uses request_params/result_params

title: The Next Generation of Audit Logging at Palantir - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Audit Logging
extractedEntities.description: Immutable record of who did what, when, and where across Palantir platforms, serving as the foundation of accountability and trust for organizations handling sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Audit.3
extractedEntities.description: Next-generation audit logging system delivering logs more quickly with more structure and accessibility, representing a fundamental reimagining of audit data capture and delivery at scale

extractedEntities.name: Privacy and Civil Liberties Engineering
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's PCL engineering team, the first of its kind, championing privacy-protective technologies as core capabilities embedded throughout the platform

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform where every action (read, write, analytical exploration, permission change) generates an audit log as a core requirement

extractedEntities.name: AIP
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's AI Platform subject to comprehensive audit logging for all AI operations and model interactions

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology operation type generating audit records for accountability across collaborative work

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism whose changes and usage patterns are captured in audit logs for insider threat detection and compliance

extractedEntities.name: Auditability by Design
extractedEntities.description: Architectural principle where auditability is a core requirement from the beginning of every new feature, not retrofitted after deployment

extractedEntities.name: Trust Through Transparency
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where comprehensive audit logging operationalizes accountability, enabling organizations to extend trust sustainably because there is a clear record of how it is used

keyInsights: Every action across Palantir platforms -- read, write, analytical exploration, or permission change -- generates an audit log; auditability is a core requirement from the start of feature development
Palantir's PCL (Privacy and Civil Liberties) engineering team was the first of its kind, establishing privacy-protective technologies as core capabilities rather than afterthoughts

The same audit logs that help security teams detect insider threats also help privacy officers ensure data minimization principles are followed -- privacy and operational effectiveness are complementary
Audit.3 represents a fundamental reimagining of how audit data is captured, delivered, and structured at scale, with logs delivered more quickly and with more structure

title: Security auditing - Monitor audit logs - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log Monitoring
extractedEntities.description: Security monitoring practice capturing who performed an action, what the action was, when it happened, and where (which resources), enabling incident response and compliance

extractedEntities.name: SIEM Integration
extractedEntities.description: External Security Information and Event Management systems that can continuously poll audit.3 logs via Palantir's audit API endpoints for low-latency detection and response

extractedEntities.name: Audit Log Delivery
extractedEntities.description: Flexible delivery mechanisms supporting external SIEM polling (audit.3 preferred) and Foundry-based dataset export for analysis

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing audit log generation and flexible delivery to external SIEMs or internal datasets for security analysis

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application used to forward audit log datasets to external SIEM platforms

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology operation whose execution is captured in audit logs with categorized type and intent information

extractedEntities.name: Audit API Endpoints
extractedEntities.description: API endpoints for audit.3: list-log-files (filters by date range) and get-log-file-content (retrieves specific log files) for SIEM integration

extractedEntities.name: Security Monitoring Workflow
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for detecting compromised accounts (impossible travel), data exfiltration (large exports outside hours), privilege escalation, and malicious code execution through audit log analysis

keyInsights: Foundry provides two audit log delivery methods: external SIEM polling via audit.3 API endpoints (preferred for low latency) and Foundry-based dataset export for lighter analytical needs
Audit log infrastructure is engineered to be append-only, ensuring audit trail integrity with aggressively restricted access to log archival storage

Key security monitoring use cases include compromised account detection (impossible travel), data exfiltration attempts (large exports outside hours), privilege escalation, and malicious code execution
Audit logs may contain PII (names, email addresses) and access should be restricted to personnel with appropriate security qualifications

title: Building Software for a Zero Trust World - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Enterprise security framework using contextual information about identities and resources to dynamically enforce policies, following DOD Zero Trust Reference Architecture tenets

extractedEntities.name: Policy Decision Point
extractedEntities.description: Component that inputs contextual information into a trust algorithm to render access decisions, exemplified by Azure Active Directory Conditional Access

extractedEntities.name: Policy Enforcement Point
extractedEntities.description: Component that enables and disables access to resources based on PDP decisions; Palantir platforms have multiple PEPs between application components and infrastructure

extractedEntities.name: Implicit Trust Zones
extractedEntities.description: Collections of secured resources between PDP and PEP; Zero Trust maturity involves shrinking these zones by adding more enforcement points

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth
extractedEntities.description: Longstanding security concept synthesized into Zero Trust, involving multiple layers of security controls

extractedEntities.name: Least Privilege
extractedEntities.description: Security principle where access is denied by default and every device, user, application, and data flow must be authenticated and authorized

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform implementing Zero Trust with multiple Policy Enforcement Points between application components and hosting infrastructure

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Implementation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern following five DOD tenets: assume hostile environment, presume breach, never trust always verify, scrutinize explicitly, and apply unified analytics

extractedEntities.name: Infrastructure-as-Code Security
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where CI enforcement validates authenticated endpoints and infrastructure-as-code configurations for security compliance

extractedEntities.name: NIST SP 800-207
extractedEntities.description: NIST standard defining Zero Trust Architecture components including Policy Decision Points and Policy Enforcement Points

keyInsights: Palantir's Zero Trust approach follows five DOD tenets: assume hostile environment, presume breach, never trust always verify, scrutinize explicitly, and apply unified analytics
Zero Trust maturity is achieved by iteratively shrinking Implicit Trust Zones -- the collections of resources between Policy Decision Points and Policy Enforcement Points
Palantir platforms implement multiple Policy Enforcement Points between application components AND in the hosting infrastructure, creating layered security
All requests are treated as untrusted until authenticated, with CI enforcement of authenticated endpoints and infrastructure-as-code security checks

title: Shared security responsibility model - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Shared Security Responsibility Model
extractedEntities.description: Framework delineating that Palantir secures the cloud infrastructure while customers are responsible for security within the cloud including data, access controls, and user identity configuration

extractedEntities.name: RBAC
extractedEntities.description: Role-Based Access Control where customers apply appropriate discretionary access controls (roles) to their Foundry resources

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access controls applied by customers to enforce security classifications and restrictions on data within Foundry

extractedEntities.name: SSO
extractedEntities.description: Single Sign-On identity provider integration required for managing user access and identity in Foundry, with mandatory multi-factor authentication

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Discretionary access control mechanism that customers must configure to control who can access and modify resources in Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform operating under a shared responsibility model where Palantir handles infrastructure security and customers handle data and access configuration

extractedEntities.name: Cloud Security Shared Responsibility
extractedEntities.description: Industry standard pattern where the cloud provider secures infrastructure (compute, storage, database, networking) while customers secure their data, applications, and access management

keyInsights: Palantir's shared responsibility model splits: Palantir secures Foundry services, encryption, network, OS/firewall, infrastructure monitoring, and global infrastructure; customers secure data, applications, IAM, resource permissions, and application-level monitoring
Customers are always responsible for: managing SSO access with MFA, ensuring uploaded data meets their policies, applying appropriate roles and markings, and monitoring audit logs
Foundry uses two types of access controls: discretionary (roles/RBAC) and mandatory (markings), both configured by customers
If customers lack an SSO provider, Palantir can provide one, but multi-factor authentication remains mandatory

title: Management - Manage granular policies - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Granular Policy
extractedEntities.description: Row-level security mechanism consisting of rules and logical operators comparing user attributes, columns/properties, and values to dynamically determine which rows or object instances a user can access

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Resource type that uses granular policies to filter which specific rows or object instances a user has permission to access

extractedEntities.name: Object Security Policy
extractedEntities.description: Policy applied to Object Types using granular policies to control instance-level access to objects

extractedEntities.name: User Attributes
extractedEntities.description: SSO-provided or manager-posted attributes (User ID, Username, Group IDs, Group Names, Organization Marking IDs) used as comparison terms in granular policies

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory access controls that propagate through the pipeline and can be applied to backing datasets of granular policies for additional protection

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity whose instances can be secured by granular policies controlling per-instance access based on user attributes and property values

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attribute on Object Types used as columns in granular policy comparisons to determine row/instance-level access

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Group-based access control that works alongside granular policies through Group IDs and Group Names in policy comparisons

extractedEntities.name: Attribute-Based Access Control
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where granular policies compare SSO-provided user attributes against data values to dynamically determine access, often the simplest policy option

extractedEntities.name: Scoped Sessions
extractedEntities.description: Advanced concept related to Authorized Group IDs in granular policies, enabling session-scoped access control

keyInsights: Granular policies are templates that compare user attributes, group memberships, and data values; when data is requested, the template converts into a query returning only permitted rows/objects
Rows with null values in policy columns become inaccessible to all users and cause issues with syncs and downstream processes like Phonograph syncs
Best practice is to handle complexity in the data pipeline rather than in the granular policy, computing dedicated policy columns to simplify policy logic
Attribute-based policies pulling from SSO or user manager are often the simplest option, supporting User ID, Username, Group IDs/Names, and Organization Marking IDs
Markings should propagate through the pipeline and can be unmarked at the resource level since the granular policy already controls row-level access

title: Action types - Permissions - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology operation type with three permission dimensions: who can view it, who can edit it, and who can apply it with a given set of parameters

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity whose edit permissions are controlled by Action Types, with a default setting that new Object Types only allow edits via actions

extractedEntities.name: Submission Criteria
extractedEntities.description: Fine-grained control mechanism determining who can run an action, supporting user ID, group ID, and parameter-based conditions

extractedEntities.name: Object Edit Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Security setting controlling whether Object Type edits are restricted to actions only (default, more secure) or also allowed via Forms, Object Explorer, and API calls

extractedEntities.name: Writeback Dataset Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Dataset-level edit permissions required when Object Types allow edits beyond actions, granting visibility to all data in the writeback dataset

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism governing who can view and edit Action Type configurations and underlying Object Types

extractedEntities.name: Restricted View
extractedEntities.description: Backing resource for Object Types where edit access requires passing the edit policy rather than dataset-level permissions

extractedEntities.name: Side Effect Permissions
extractedEntities.description: Any user who can set up an action may configure side effects; webhook side effects require additional permissions and are not enabled by default

extractedEntities.name: Action-Only Edits
extractedEntities.description: Default security pattern where new Object Types only allow edits via Actions, requiring only Read access on objects being edited and enforcing consistent security across workflows

keyInsights: By default, new Object Types only allow edits via Actions, requiring only Read access on objects being edited -- this is the recommended security paradigm
Opening Object Types to edits beyond actions (Forms, Object Explorer, API) requires Edit permissions on writeback datasets, potentially exposing more data than necessary
Action Type configuration does NOT display permission settings on affected underlying Object Types; configurators must independently verify permissions
Submission criteria enable fine-grained action execution control combining user ID, group ID, and action parameter conditions
Users can create objects they cannot view when the action-only edit mode is enabled, since only Read access is required on edited objects

title: Authentication - API Reference - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: OAuth 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Authentication and authorization protocol used by all Foundry APIs, providing access through bearer tokens, authorization code grants, and client credentials grants

extractedEntities.name: Bearer Token
extractedEntities.description: API authentication string passed as Authorization header using Bearer scheme, giving token owner access to Foundry resources with the permissions of the generating user

extractedEntities.name: Authorization Code Grant
extractedEntities.description: OAuth2 grant allowing applications to act on behalf of users by redirecting to Foundry for standard authentication, producing short-lived access tokens

extractedEntities.name: Client Credentials Grant
extractedEntities.description: OAuth2 grant allowing applications to act as a service user using a stored client secret to request short-lived access tokens

extractedEntities.name: OAuth2 Scope
extractedEntities.description: Permission specification during authorization representing the intersection of requested scope and existing user/service user permissions in Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform providing OAuth2-based API authentication with Developer Console for grant configuration

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application where OAuth2 grants are configured during application setup

extractedEntities.name: API Scopes
extractedEntities.description: Endpoint-specific permission scopes: api:ontologies-read for reads, api:ontologies-write for writes, and both for read-write operations

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism whose permissions intersect with OAuth2 scope to determine effective API access

keyInsights: All Foundry APIs use OAuth 2.0 with two grant types: Authorization Code (acts on behalf of users) and Client Credentials (acts as service user)
Effective API access is the intersection of the OAuth2 scope requested and the existing permissions of the user or service user in Foundry
User-generated tokens hold the same permissions as the creating user and should never be used in production -- production apps must use OAuth2
API scopes are endpoint-specific: api:ontologies-read for reads, api:ontologies-write for writes, with both needed for read-write operations

title: Protecting tokens and API keys - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: User-Generated Token
extractedEntities.description: Personal access token granting user-level Foundry access with configurable TTL; security property that it cannot generate other user-generated tokens (only session tokens can)

extractedEntities.name: Session Token
extractedEntities.description: Ephemeral time-bound token stored as browser cookie (PALANTIR_TOKEN) with default 16-hour TTL, the only token type that can create new user-generated tokens

extractedEntities.name: Data Connector Coordinator Token
extractedEntities.description: Token authenticating data connector agents with Foundry, stored in bootvisor directory, with time-bound tokens dynamically inserted into deployment tarballs

extractedEntities.name: Token Spill Response
extractedEntities.description: Security incident response procedure for exposed tokens: immediately disable the token, then notify information security team

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform using three token types (user-generated, session, data connector coordinator) each with distinct security properties and lifetimes

extractedEntities.name: Token Storage Paths
extractedEntities.description: Data connector coordinator token stored at {BOOTVISOR_DIRECTORY}/var/data/coordinator-token.json; session token stored as PALANTIR_TOKEN browser cookie

extractedEntities.name: Token Security Best Practices
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of never sharing tokens, using environment variables for local development, excluding dotfiles from version control, applying reasonable TTL values, and revoking unused tokens

keyInsights: Foundry uses three distinct token types: user-generated (configurable TTL, cannot create other tokens), session (16-hour default, stored as PALANTIR_TOKEN cookie), and data connector coordinator
User-generated tokens have a critical security property: they cannot generate other user-generated tokens, only session tokens can create new personal access tokens
Token spills should be treated as information security incidents requiring immediate token revocation followed by security team notification
Data connector deployment tarballs contain time-bound authentication tokens and should be treated as sensitive material

title: Protecting identity - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: SSO Integration
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's identity protection supporting SAML 2.0 and OIDC 1.0 protocols for single sign-on with enterprise identity providers

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Factor Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Mandatory security requirement for Foundry access, with strong forms ranked: hardware tokens (FIDO2/CAC), disconnected OTP generators, software authenticators, biometrics

extractedEntities.name: FIDO2 Passkeys
extractedEntities.description: Passwordless authentication mechanism supported by Foundry using connected hardware tokens like YubiKey and Google Titan USB security keys

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust Identity
extractedEntities.description: Identity-focused Zero Trust principles requiring conditional access, device health assessments, strong MFA claims, and not trusting weak indicators like network location

extractedEntities.name: Service Account Management
extractedEntities.description: Security practice for managing broadly-permissive service accounts that are attractive targets for adversaries due to poor securing compared to standard user accounts

extractedEntities.name: Roles
extractedEntities.description: Access control mechanism supported by provider group mirroring and JIT user provisioning from SSO identity providers

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform enforcing short maximum session lifetimes to force periodic re-authentication, with native MFA support for environments without identity provider MFA

extractedEntities.name: SAML 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Security Assertion Markup Language protocol supported for Foundry SSO integration with enterprise identity providers

extractedEntities.name: OIDC 1.0
extractedEntities.description: OpenID Connect protocol supported for Foundry SSO integration alongside SAML 2.0

extractedEntities.name: Identity Security Hierarchy
extractedEntities.description: MFA strength ranking from strongest to weakest: connected hardware tokens (FIDO2), disconnected OTP generators, software authenticators, biometrics; SMS and email OTP are explicitly not recommended

keyInsights: Foundry supports SAML 2.0 and OIDC 1.0 for SSO with mandatory multi-factor authentication, and provides native MFA if the identity provider lacks it
MFA strength is explicitly ranked: FIDO2/CAC hardware tokens > disconnected OTP generators > software authenticators > biometrics; SMS and email OTP are explicitly discouraged
Foundry intentionally mandates short maximum session lifetimes to force periodic re-authentication, since stolen session tokens can be abused for their full duration
Zero Trust identity practices include not trusting weak indicators (software certificates, single-factor auth, network location) and requiring device health attestations
Service accounts require strict management because they typically have broad permissions and are poorly secured compared to standard user accounts, making them attractive attack targets
